event: Early humans of the species Homo antecessor inhabit the Atapuerca region, among the oldest hominin remains in Western Europe.
Paleolithic artists paint the renowned cave art of Altamira depicting bison and other animals.
Phoenician traders found Gadir (modern Cadiz), among the oldest cities in Western Europe.
Greek colonists establish trading settlements such as Emporion on the Mediterranean coast.
Carthage under the Barcid family begins large-scale conquest of southern and eastern Iberia.
Rome lands at Emporion, opening the Iberian theater of the Second Punic War against Carthage.
Rome defeats Carthage at Ilipa, ending Carthaginian power in Iberia.
Emperor Augustus completes the Roman conquest of Iberia by ending the Cantabrian Wars.
Emperor Vespasian grants Latin rights to the towns of Hispania, accelerating Romanization.
Suebi, Vandals, and Alans cross the Pyrenees and settle in the Iberian Peninsula amid Roman collapse.
The Visigoths, defeated at Vouille, shift the center of their kingdom into Hispania.
King Reccared converts from Arianism to Catholicism at the Third Council of Toledo, unifying Visigothic religion.
Umayyad forces under Tariq ibn Ziyad defeat King Roderic, beginning the Muslim conquest of Iberia.
Pelagius's victory at Covadonga founds the Kingdom of Asturias and traditionally begins the Reconquista.
Abd al-Rahman I establishes the independent Emirate of Cordoba.
Abd al-Rahman III proclaims the Caliphate of Cordoba, the peak of Al-Andalus's power and culture.
The Caliphate of Cordoba collapses into rival taifa kingdoms.
Alfonso VI of Leon and Castile captures Toledo, a major Reconquista milestone.
Christian kingdoms win the decisive Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa, breaking Almohad power in Iberia.
Ferdinand III of Castile captures Seville, leaving Granada as the last Muslim state in Iberia.
The marriage of Isabella of Castile and Ferdinand of Aragon unites the two crowns.
The Catholic Monarchs establish the Spanish Inquisition to enforce religious orthodoxy.
Granada falls, Jews are expelled, and Columbus reaches the Americas under Spanish sponsorship.
Charles I becomes king, soon ruling as Holy Roman Emperor Charles V over a vast Habsburg empire.
Philip II makes Madrid the permanent capital of Spain.
The Spanish Armada is defeated in its attempt to invade England.
Miguel de Cervantes publishes the first part of Don Quixote, a foundational work of modern literature.
Charles II dies childless, triggering the War of the Spanish Succession and the Bourbon accession.
Barcelona falls and the peace treaties install Philip V, founding Spain's Bourbon dynasty.
Napoleon installs his brother as king, igniting the Peninsular War and Spanish War of Independence.
The Cortes of Cadiz promulgates Spain's first liberal constitution.
Most of Spain's mainland American colonies achieve independence after years of revolution.
The short-lived First Spanish Republic is proclaimed amid political turmoil.
Defeat in the Spanish-American War costs Spain Cuba, Puerto Rico, and the Philippines.
General Miguel Primo de Rivera seizes power, establishing a military dictatorship.
King Alfonso XIII goes into exile and the Second Spanish Republic is proclaimed.
A military uprising against the Republic ignites the Spanish Civil War.
Franco's Nationalists win the Civil War, beginning a long authoritarian dictatorship.
Spain is admitted to the United Nations, ending postwar diplomatic isolation.
Franco dies and Juan Carlos I becomes king, opening Spain's transition to democracy.
Spaniards approve a new democratic constitution establishing a parliamentary monarchy.
A coup attempt in the Cortes fails after the king publicly defends democracy.
Spain joins the European Economic Community, the forerunner of the European Union.
Barcelona hosts the Summer Olympics and Seville the World Expo, showcasing modern Spain.
Spain adopts the euro as its national currency, retiring the peseta.
Madrid train bombings kill 193 people in Spain's deadliest terrorist attack.
The global financial crisis triggers a severe Spanish recession with banking and housing collapse.
King Juan Carlos I abdicates and his son Felipe VI ascends the throne.
Catalonia's unilateral independence referendum and declaration provoke a major constitutional crisis.
